+## Stateless Mode
+
+For Kubernetes deployments or environments where session state should be ephemeral, use the `--stateless` flag with streamable-http transport:
+
+```bash
+# Using uvx
+uvx mcp-atlassian --transport streamable-http --stateless --port 9000 -vv
+
+# Or using Docker
+docker run --rm -p 9000:9000 \
+  --env-file /path/to/your/.env \
+  ghcr.io/sooperset/mcp-atlassian:latest \
+  --transport streamable-http --stateless --port 9000 -vv
+
+# Or via environment variable
+STATELESS=true uvx mcp-atlassian --transport streamable-http --port 9000
+```
+
+<Note>
+The `--stateless` flag is only supported with `streamable-http` transport. Using it with `stdio` or `sse` will result in an error.
+</Note>
+

problem_statement:
[Bug]: Default Project Filter Causes Invalid JQL When No Clause Is Provided
### Prerequisites

- [x] I have searched the [existing issues](https://github.com/sooperset/mcp-atlassian/issues) to make sure this bug has not already been reported.
- [x] I have checked the [README](https://github.com/sooperset/mcp-atlassian/blob/main/README.md) for relevant information.

### Bug Description

When I call the `search` tool (Jira issue search) without specifying a project in the JQL, and rely on the `JIRA_PROJECTS_FILTER` environment variable to set the default project(s), the tool fails with a JQL syntax error if the JQL is only an `ORDER BY` clause or is otherwise empty.

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Set the environment variable `JIRA_PROJECTS_FILTER=PROJ1,PROJ2` (or any valid project keys).
2. Start the MCP server and ensure it is using this environment variable.
3. Call the Jira `search` tool with a JQL like:
   - `ORDER BY created DESC`
   - or leave the JQL empty (just want the most recent issues from the default projects).
4. Observe the error returned.

### Expected Behavior

The MCP server should automatically prepend the default project filter to the JQL, resulting in a valid query like:
```
project IN ("PROJ1", "PROJ2") ORDER BY created DESC
```
and return the most recent issues from the allowed projects.

### Actual Behavior

```shell
The MCP server constructs an invalid JQL:

(ORDER BY created DESC) AND project IN ("ACQ", "LMAN")

This results in a Jira error:

Error in the JQL Query: Expecting a field name but got 'ORDER'. You must surround 'ORDER' in quotation marks to use it as a field name.

or similar JQL syntax errors.
```

### mcp-atlassian Version

0.11.8

### Installation Method

Docker

### Operating System

macOS

### Python Version

3.10.18

### Atlassian Instance Type

Jira Server / Data Center

### Client Application

Cursor

### Additional Context

- The bug is in the logic that injects the project filter into the JQL. It always tries to `AND` the project filter, even if the JQL is just an `ORDER BY` clause or empty.
- If the JQL is empty, the tool works (returns all issues from the default projects, but unsorted).
- If the JQL already contains a project clause, the tool works as expected.
- The bug prevents users from getting sorted results from the default projects unless they manually specify the project(s) in the JQL.

Suggested Fix
Update the JQL construction logic to detect when the JQL is empty or starts with `ORDER BY`, and prepend the project filter instead of trying to `AND` it. For example:
- If JQL is empty: `project IN ("ACQ", "LMAN")`
- If JQL starts with `ORDER BY`: `project IN ("ACQ", "LMAN") ORDER BY ...`
- Otherwise, use the current logic.

problem_statement:
[Bug]: jira_transition_issue fields parameter not working - cannot set resolution during transition
### Prerequisites

- [x] I have searched the [existing issues](https://github.com/sooperset/mcp-atlassian/issues) to make sure this bug has not already been reported.
- [x] I have checked the [README](https://github.com/sooperset/mcp-atlassian/blob/main/README.md) for relevant information.

### Bug Description

The `jira_transition_issue` function successfully changes issue status but appears to ignore the `fields` parameter when provided. When attempting to set the resolution field during a transition to a closed status (like "Rejected"), the status changes correctly but the resolution field remains unset. This prevents proper automation of issue closure workflows that require specific resolution values.

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Use `jira_transition_issue` with both `transition_id` and `fields` parameters:
```python
   mcp_atlassian___jira_transition_issue(
       issue_key="PROJ-123",
       transition_id="111",  # Example: Reject transition
       fields={"resolution": {"id": "10001"}}  # Example: Won't Do resolution
   )
```
2. Verify the issue was transitioned by checking its status
3. Check if the resolution field was set during the transition
4. Compare with direct REST API call using same parameters

### Expected Behavior

When using `jira_transition_issue` with both `transition_id` and `fields` parameters:
• The issue status should change according to the transition
• The fields specified in the `fields` parameter should be updated during the transition
• Specifically, the resolution field should be set when transitioning to closed statuses
• The behavior should match what happens when calling the Jira REST API directly

### Actual Behavior

• The transition succeeds and changes the issue status correctly
• The `fields` parameter appears to be completely ignored
• No resolution (or other fields) are set on the issue
• No error messages or warnings are returned
• The function reports success despite not setting the requested fields

### mcp-atlassian Version

0.11.9

### Installation Method

Docker

### Operating System

macOS

### Python Version

Python 3.10.18 (running inside Docker container)

### Atlassian Instance Type

Jira Cloud

### Client Application

Amazon Q CLI with MCP integration

### Additional Context

I'm open to the possibility that this isn't a bug and I'm doing something wrong. But I can't get the AI to make the tool perform the transition with resolution.

#### mcp-atlassian version
• Version: 0.11.9
• Docker image: ghcr.io/sooperset/mcp-atlassian:latest
• Image created: 2025-06-29T13:59:44.711Z
• Git revision: 3b07fd3d05f88cd18c37a1739d4e0a1f37fa9aa5

#### Verification with Direct API
The same operation works correctly when called directly via Jira REST API:
```bash
curl -X POST \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -u "$JIRA_EMAIL:$JIRA_API_TOKEN" \
  -d '{
    "transition": {"id": "111"},
    "fields": {"resolution": {"id": "10001"}}
  }' \
  "https://company.atlassian.net/rest/api/2/issue/PROJ-123/transitions"
```

This successfully sets both the status and resolution field as expected.

#### Environment Details
• Jira instance: Atlassian Cloud
• Issue workflow: Standard Jira workflow
• Network: Corporate environment (no special proxy configuration affecting this issue)
• Authentication: API token-based authentication

#### Use Case
This functionality is needed for bulk automation that close old issues with specific resolution values (like "Won't Do", "Cancelled", etc.). The ability to set resolution during transition is a common workflow requirement and is supported by the underlying Jira REST API.

@@ -0,0 +1,75 @@
+"""OAuth proxy extensions and configuration helpers."""
+
+from __future__ import annotations
+
+import logging
+from collections.abc import Iterable
+
+from fastmcp.server.auth.oauth_proxy import OAuthProxy
+from mcp.server.auth.provider import OAuthClientInformationFull
+
+logger = logging.getLogger("mcp-atlassian.server.oauth_proxy")
+
+
+def _normalize_list(values: Iterable[str] | None) -> list[str] | None:
+    if values is None:
+        return None
+    return [value.strip() for value in values if value and value.strip()]
+
+
+def parse_env_list(raw: str | None) -> list[str] | None:
+    if raw is None:
+        return None
+    if not raw.strip():
+        return []
+    normalized = raw.replace(",", " ")
+    return [item.strip() for item in normalized.split() if item.strip()]
+
+
+class HardenedOAuthProxy(OAuthProxy):
+    """OAuthProxy with stricter DCR controls for grants and scopes."""
+
+    def __init__(
+        self,
+        *,
+        allowed_grant_types: list[str] | None = None,
+        forced_scopes: list[str] | None = None,
+        **kwargs: object,
+    ) -> None:
+        super().__init__(**kwargs)
+        self._allowed_grant_types = _normalize_list(allowed_grant_types)
+        self._forced_scopes = _normalize_list(forced_scopes)
+
+    async def register_client(self, client_info: OAuthClientInformationFull) -> None:
+        updates: dict[str, object] = {"response_types": ["code"]}
+
+        if self._allowed_grant_types is not None:
+            requested = list(client_info.grant_types or [])
+            filtered = [gt for gt in requested if gt in self._allowed_grant_types]
+            if requested and set(requested) - set(filtered):
+                logger.warning(
+                    "DCR requested unsupported grant types %s; enforcing %s",
+                    sorted(set(requested) - set(filtered)),
+                    self._allowed_grant_types,
+                )
+            if not filtered:
+                filtered = list(self._allowed_grant_types)
+            updates["grant_types"] = filtered
+
+        if self._forced_scopes is not None:
+            forced_scope = " ".join(self._forced_scopes).strip()
+            updates["scope"] = forced_scope or None
+            if client_info.scope and client_info.scope != forced_scope:
+                logger.warning(
+                    "DCR requested scope '%s'; enforcing '%s'",
+                    client_info.scope,
+                    forced_scope,
+                )
+
+        if updates:
+            client_info = client_info.model_copy(update=updates)
+
+        await super().register_client(client_info)
+
+
+__all__ = ["HardenedOAuthProxy", "parse_env_list"]

@@ -0,0 +1,29 @@
+"""Token verifier for Atlassian opaque OAuth tokens.
+
+FastMCP's OAuthProxy requires a TokenVerifier for loaded upstream access tokens.
+Atlassian OAuth tokens are opaque in many environments and there is no stable
+JWKS endpoint for verification, so we accept non-empty tokens and attach the
+required scopes.
+"""
+
+from __future__ import annotations
+
+import time
+
+from fastmcp.server.auth.auth import AccessToken, TokenVerifier
+
+
+class AtlassianOpaqueTokenVerifier(TokenVerifier):
+    """Accept opaque Atlassian tokens and wrap them in AccessToken."""
+
+    async def verify_token(self, token: str) -> AccessToken | None:  # noqa: D401
+        if not token:
+            return None
+
+        scopes = self.required_scopes or []
+        return AccessToken(
+            token=token,
+            client_id="atlassian",
+            scopes=scopes,
+            expires_at=int(time.time()) + 86400 * 30,
+        )

problem_statement:
[Feature]: Enabling multi-user OAuth flow for self-hosted remote MCP (like Atlassian-hosted remote MCP)
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

Hi team,

We’re trying to replicate the behavior described in [this Atlassian community post](https://community.atlassian.com/forums/Atlassian-Platform-articles/Using-the-Atlassian-Remote-MCP-Server-beta/ba-p/3005104) using our **own self-hosted remote MCP server**, which we currently host as an ECS service.

When using Atlassian’s hosted remote MCP at `https://mcp.atlassian.com/v1/sse`, users are automatically redirected to the OAuth 2.0 wizard, where they can authenticate and grant access. This per-user OAuth flow works great — each user is identified individually, and tokens are handled dynamically.

### Describe the solution you'd like

Our goal is to enable **the same behavior** in our self-hosted remote MCP, where:

- Users accessing the MCP (via **Claude Desktop**) are automatically redirected to the Atlassian OAuth wizard if they haven’t authenticated yet
- Each user goes through the OAuth 2.0 flow and obtains their own access token
- Those tokens are stored and scoped based on their Atlassian permissions (projects, spaces, etc.)
- Authorization is **per user**, not globally tied to the deployer

Currently, when setting up a remote MCP, the workflow relies on a one-time manual auth to obtain tokens. We’d like to know if the MCP codebase supports (or plans to support) this **multi-user OAuth model** for self-hosted deployments.

Is there a way to configure the app so that self-hosted remote MCPs behave the same way as `https://mcp.atlassian.com/v1/sse`, prompting each user with their own auth flow?

Any guidance or best practices would be appreciated!

Thanks in advance.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

FYI @sooperset 
Hey everyone,

A bit late to the party here.  We ended up solving essentially this “multi-user OAuth for a self-hosted remote MCP” by making the MCP server itself act as a small OAuth 2.1 authorization server (per the MCP OAuth/DCR spec), and then proxying to Atlassian (Jira/Confluence) OAuth under the hood.

High-level: the MCP client (ChatGPT/Claude Desktop/etc) does DCR + PKCE against our MCP server, and our MCP server then performs the upstream Atlassian OAuth flow and stores per-user Atlassian tokens. The MCP client never needs Atlassian secrets, and tokens are per-user (not a global “deployer token”).

**Pieces I had to add/change**

  - Upgrade FastMCP (security + API changes, incl. support for [OAuth Proxy](https://gofastmcp.com/servers/auth/oauth-proxy) and [Storage Backends](https://gofastmcp.com/servers/storage-backends) based on [py-key-value-aio](https://github.com/strawgate/py-key-value)) and refactor request context handling so auth state can be resolved per request/user.
  - Add an OAuth Proxy layer that:
      - exposes `/.well-known/oauth-protected-resource` + `/.well-known/oauth-authorization-server` as per [MCP AuthZ/AuthN spec](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25/basic/authorization)
      - implements POST /register (Dynamic Client Registration)
      - implements GET /authorize (PKCE auth code flow + user consent)
      - implements POST /token (code + refresh exchange for MCP access tokens)
      - implements GET /callback (upstream Atlassian redirect target)
  - Add a storage backend for:
      - DCR client registrations (`client_id`/`client_secret`, `redirect_uri`s, etc)
      - per-user upstream tokens (Atlassian access/refresh tokens), keyed by our “subject”
      - We used in-memory for local dev + a persistent KV backend (JetStream/NATS KV) in k8s; could be swapped for Redis/SQLite/etc.
  - Implement the token swap / bridging:
      - On callback, exchange Atlassian code at Atlassian token endpoint
      - Store upstream token set
      - Issue our own MCP access/refresh token back to the MCP client
      - On tool calls, verify our token → load upstream token → call Jira/Confluence

**Important allow-lists (common pitfall)**
  - Atlassian DC/Cloud OAuth app must allow-list the MCP server callback:
      - `https://<your-host>/<your-mcp-base>/callback`
  - The MCP server must allow-list the MCP client redirect URI in DCR:
      - ChatGPT: `https://chatgpt.com/connector_platform_oauth_redirect`
      - (Claude Desktop / others have their own)

**Flow (sequence diagram)**

```mermaid
  sequenceDiagram
    participant C as MCP Client (ChatGPT/Claude)
    participant M as Self-hosted MCP (OAuth AS + Proxy)
    participant A as Atlassian (Jira/Confluence OAuth)
    participant S as Token/Client Store

    C->>M: GET /.well-known/oauth-protected-resource
    C->>M: GET /.well-known/oauth-authorization-server
    C->>M: POST /register (DCR: redirect_uris, grant_types, scope)
    M->>S: persist client registration
    C->>M: GET /authorize (PKCE)
    M->>C: consent UI (optional)
    M->>A: 302 -> Atlassian /authorize (client_id=<ATLASSIAN_APP_ID>, redirect_uri=<M/callback>)
    A->>M: GET /callback?code=... (user authenticated)
    M->>A: POST /token (swap code for Atlassian tokens)
    M->>S: store per-user Atlassian tokens
    M->>C: 302 -> client redirect_uri with MCP auth code
    C->>M: POST /token (code + client_secret + verifier)
    M->>C: MCP access_token (+ refresh_token)
    C->>M: MCP tool calls w/ Authorization: Bearer <MCP token>
    M->>S: lookup upstream tokens
    M->>A: call Jira/Confluence APIs as that user
```

**Would this be PR-able upstream?**
I think yes, but it’s a non-trivial feature: it adds an OAuth AS surface + storage + per-request auth resolution. If @sooperset  / maintainers are interested, I'd suggest keeping it fully opt-in (feature flag/env vars) and structured as:

  - a generic “MCP OAuth server” module (DCR/PKCE/token endpoints + storage interface)
  - an Atlassian-specific “upstream provider” (DC vs Cloud endpoints, accessible-resources/cloud_id nuances, scopes)
  - docs + an example “remote MCP for multiple users” deployment

Happy to share more details / open a PR if this direction aligns with the project’s goals.
@Poggen You're saying you made the MCP server into an OAuth authorization server ?

If so, it shouldn't be this complicated. Per the MCP spec, MCP servers are supposed to be OAuth resource servers, meaning they should be able to return a 401 when first accessed, and provide a WWW-Authenticate header pointing to the PRM endpoint (.well-known/outh-protected-resource). Then, a proper OAuth client (Claude Desktop, Gemini CLI, whatever) should be able to pick this response up, inspect the WWW-Authenticate header and send a request to the PRM endpoint to get information about the authorization server (Jira, in this case), which it will use the authenticate a get a valid token.  

So the only thing the MCP server is supposed to do is return a 401 with the right header and you're all set. This shouldn't be too difficult to implement.

Note : specifically in the case of Jira Data Center, only problem is : Jira doesn't seem to properly comply with RFC 8414 regarding the URL at which it exposes the auth server metadata. But this is out of scope.
@ragaoua thanks for your reply — that is helpful info. I agree that MCP servers should do proper OAuth discovery (`401` + `WWW-Authenticate` + protected resource metadata).

I think we may be talking about two different deployment models though, so I’d like to clarify:

1. Are you suggesting the MCP client should authenticate directly against Jira/Confluence as the authorization server, with no MCP-side auth server/proxy at all?
2. If yes, how do you handle Jira Data Center not exposing standard metadata/DCR endpoints in a way MCP clients can consume directly?
3. In that model, where do you keep the Atlassian OAuth client credentials (especially `client_secret`) for multi-user remote MCP, without pushing that burden to each MCP client?
4. How do you handle per-user token refresh and token-to-user mapping in a way that still satisfies MCP security guidance (no token passthrough)?
5. Do you have a concrete end-to-end example (ChatGPT/Claude Desktop/Gemini CLI) working with only the 401 discovery challenge and no MCP-side token bridge?

If you have a working reference flow, I’d genuinely like to align with it and simplify my frankenstein above 🙈
After rebasing onto recent upstream versions and FastMCP updates, we were able to remove a large amount of custom auth glue in our fork. So the remaining gap is now very specific to MCP-spec compliant, per-user OAuth for with DCR, which for example is needed to support clients such as ChatGPT & Codex.

Concretely, this means implementing the missing discovery/registration flow so MCP clients can authenticate users without out-of-band token setup:

1. `401` + `WWW-Authenticate`
2. `/.well-known/oauth-protected-resource`
3. `/.well-known/oauth-authorization-server`
4. `POST /register` (DCR)
5. `GET /authorize` (PKCE)
6. `POST /token`
7. `GET /callback` (upstream Atlassian redirect target)

What this enables:
1. Per-user OAuth for self-hosted remote deployments.
2. Spec-aligned onboarding for MCP clients via discovery + DCR.
3. No requirement to pre-provision per-user bearer tokens manually in client config.

This aligns with both this issue and #556. I can contribute this as one focused PR with backward compatibility for existing PAT/basic/header-based flows, plus tests/docs/e2e coverage.

Hey everyone,

A bit late to the party here.  We ended up solving essentially this “multi-user OAuth for a self-hosted remote MCP” by making the MCP server itself act as a small OAuth 2.1 authorization server (per the MCP OAuth/DCR spec), and then proxying to Atlassian (Jira/Confluence) OAuth under the hood.

High-level: the MCP client (ChatGPT/Claude Desktop/etc) does DCR + PKCE against our MCP server, and our MCP server then performs the upstream Atlassian OAuth flow and stores per-user Atlassian tokens. The MCP client never needs Atlassian secrets, and tokens are per-user (not a global “deployer token”).

**Pieces I had to add/change**

  - Upgrade FastMCP (security + API changes, incl. support for [OAuth Proxy](https://gofastmcp.com/servers/auth/oauth-proxy) and [Storage Backends](https://gofastmcp.com/servers/storage-backends) based on [py-key-value-aio](https://github.com/strawgate/py-key-value)) and refactor request context handling so auth state can be resolved per request/user.
  - Add an OAuth Proxy layer that:
      - exposes `/.well-known/oauth-protected-resource` + `/.well-known/oauth-authorization-server` as per [MCP AuthZ/AuthN spec](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25/basic/authorization)
      - implements POST /register (Dynamic Client Registration)
      - implements GET /authorize (PKCE auth code flow + user consent)
      - implements POST /token (code + refresh exchange for MCP access tokens)
      - implements GET /callback (upstream Atlassian redirect target)
  - Add a storage backend for:
      - DCR client registrations (`client_id`/`client_secret`, `redirect_uri`s, etc)
      - per-user upstream tokens (Atlassian access/refresh tokens), keyed by our “subject”
      - We used in-memory for local dev + a persistent KV backend (JetStream/NATS KV) in k8s; could be swapped for Redis/SQLite/etc.
  - Implement the token swap / bridging:
      - On callback, exchange Atlassian code at Atlassian token endpoint
      - Store upstream token set
      - Issue our own MCP access/refresh token back to the MCP client
      - On tool calls, verify our token → load upstream token → call Jira/Confluence

**Important allow-lists (common pitfall)**
  - Atlassian DC/Cloud OAuth app must allow-list the MCP server callback:
      - `https://<your-host>/<your-mcp-base>/callback`
  - The MCP server must allow-list the MCP client redirect URI in DCR:
      - ChatGPT: `https://chatgpt.com/connector_platform_oauth_redirect`
      - (Claude Desktop / others have their own)

**Flow (sequence diagram)**

```mermaid
  sequenceDiagram
    participant C as MCP Client (ChatGPT/Claude)
    participant M as Self-hosted MCP (OAuth AS + Proxy)
    participant A as Atlassian (Jira/Confluence OAuth)
    participant S as Token/Client Store

    C->>M: GET /.well-known/oauth-protected-resource
    C->>M: GET /.well-known/oauth-authorization-server
    C->>M: POST /register (DCR: redirect_uris, grant_types, scope)
    M->>S: persist client registration
    C->>M: GET /authorize (PKCE)
    M->>C: consent UI (optional)
    M->>A: 302 -> Atlassian /authorize (client_id=<ATLASSIAN_APP_ID>, redirect_uri=<M/callback>)
    A->>M: GET /callback?code=... (user authenticated)
    M->>A: POST /token (swap code for Atlassian tokens)
    M->>S: store per-user Atlassian tokens
    M->>C: 302 -> client redirect_uri with MCP auth code
    C->>M: POST /token (code + client_secret + verifier)
    M->>C: MCP access_token (+ refresh_token)
    C->>M: MCP tool calls w/ Authorization: Bearer <MCP token>
    M->>S: lookup upstream tokens
    M->>A: call Jira/Confluence APIs as that user
```

**Would this be PR-able upstream?**
I think yes, but it’s a non-trivial feature: it adds an OAuth AS surface + storage + per-request auth resolution. If @sooperset  / maintainers are interested, I'd suggest keeping it fully opt-in (feature flag/env vars) and structured as:

  - a generic “MCP OAuth server” module (DCR/PKCE/token endpoints + storage interface)
  - an Atlassian-specific “upstream provider” (DC vs Cloud endpoints, accessible-resources/cloud_id nuances, scopes)
  - docs + an example “remote MCP for multiple users” deployment

Happy to share more details / open a PR if this direction aligns with the project’s goals.
@Poggen You're saying you made the MCP server into an OAuth authorization server ?

If so, it shouldn't be this complicated. Per the MCP spec, MCP servers are supposed to be OAuth resource servers, meaning they should be able to return a 401 when first accessed, and provide a WWW-Authenticate header pointing to the PRM endpoint (.well-known/outh-protected-resource). Then, a proper OAuth client (Claude Desktop, Gemini CLI, whatever) should be able to pick this response up, inspect the WWW-Authenticate header and send a request to the PRM endpoint to get information about the authorization server (Jira, in this case), which it will use the authenticate a get a valid token.  

So the only thing the MCP server is supposed to do is return a 401 with the right header and you're all set. This shouldn't be too difficult to implement.

Note : specifically in the case of Jira Data Center, only problem is : Jira doesn't seem to properly comply with RFC 8414 regarding the URL at which it exposes the auth server metadata. But this is out of scope.

After rebasing onto recent upstream versions and FastMCP updates, we were able to remove a large amount of custom auth glue in our fork. So the remaining gap is now very specific to MCP-spec compliant, per-user OAuth for with DCR, which for example is needed to support clients such as ChatGPT & Codex.

Concretely, this means implementing the missing discovery/registration flow so MCP clients can authenticate users without out-of-band token setup:

1. `401` + `WWW-Authenticate`
2. `/.well-known/oauth-protected-resource`
3. `/.well-known/oauth-authorization-server`
4. `POST /register` (DCR)
5. `GET /authorize` (PKCE)
6. `POST /token`
7. `GET /callback` (upstream Atlassian redirect target)

What this enables:
1. Per-user OAuth for self-hosted remote deployments.
2. Spec-aligned onboarding for MCP clients via discovery + DCR.
3. No requirement to pre-provision per-user bearer tokens manually in client config.

+    def _prepare_epic_link_fields(
+        self, fields: dict[str, Any], kwargs: dict[str, Any]
+    ) -> None:
+        """Resolve epic link aliases (epicKey, epic_link, etc.) to the actual custom field ID.
+
+        Checks kwargs for known epic link aliases, discovers the real
+        custom field ID via ``get_field_ids_to_epic()``, and sets it in
+        *fields*.  On Cloud, falls back to the ``parent`` field when no
+        epic link custom field is discovered (team-managed projects use
+        parent for epic relationships).
+
+        Args:
+            fields: The issue fields dict (mutated in place).
+            kwargs: Caller-provided keyword arguments (matched alias is popped).
+        """
+        epic_key_value = None
+        matched_alias = None
+        for key in list(kwargs.keys()):
+            if key.lower() in _EPIC_LINK_ALIASES:
+                epic_key_value = kwargs.pop(key)
+                matched_alias = key
+                break
+
+        if not epic_key_value:
+            return
+
+        # Discover the epic link custom field ID
+        try:
+            field_ids = self.get_field_ids_to_epic()
+            epic_link_field_id = field_ids.get("epic_link")
+        except Exception as e:
+            logger.debug(f"Could not discover epic link field: {e}")
+            epic_link_field_id = None
+
+        if epic_link_field_id:
+            fields[epic_link_field_id] = epic_key_value
+            logger.info(
+                f"Set epic link field {epic_link_field_id}={epic_key_value} "
+                f"from alias '{matched_alias}'"
+            )
+        elif self.config.is_cloud and "parent" not in fields:
+            fields["parent"] = {"key": epic_key_value}
+            logger.info(
+                f"No epic link field found, using parent field for "
+                f"epic link '{epic_key_value}' (Cloud fallback)"
+            )
+        else:
+            logger.warning(
+                f"Could not resolve epic link alias '{matched_alias}'="
+                f"{epic_key_value}. No epic link custom field discovered. "
+                f"Try using the exact custom field ID (e.g., customfield_10014)."
+            )
+

[Bug]: user auth_type is 'oauth' but i'm in server / data center config
### Prerequisites

- [x] I have searched the [existing issues](https://github.com/sooperset/mcp-atlassian/issues) to make sure this bug has not already been reported.
- [x] I have checked the [README](https://github.com/sooperset/mcp-atlassian/blob/main/README.md) for relevant information.

### Bug Description

Hello,

When I try to run my Confluence tools, I consistently encounter the following error:
**`ValueError: Global OAuth config for ConfluenceConfig is missing, but user auth_type is 'oauth'`.**

This error only occurs at runtime — **not** when connecting to the MCP server or when listing available tools. It happens specifically during the connection to Confluence.

I’m using a **private Confluence instance**, which is why I’ve configured it in **Server/Data Center mode with a Personal Access Token (PAT)**.
Here’s an excerpt from my `docker-compose.yml`, using the variables mentioned in the documentation:

```yaml
mcp-atlassian:
  image: ghcr.io/sooperset/mcp-atlassian:latest
  container_name: mcp-atlassian-confluence
  environment:
    CONFLUENCE_URL: ${CONFLUENCE_URL:-}
    CONFLUENCE_PERSONAL_TOKEN: ${CONFLUENCE_PERSONAL_TOKEN:-}
    CONFLUENCE_SSL_VERIFY: ${CONFLUENCE_SSL_VERIFY:-false}
    ENABLED_TOOLS: "confluence_search"
  command: 
    - "--transport"
    - "streamable-http"
    - "--port"
    - "9000"
    - "--enabled-tools"
    - "confluence_search,confluence_get_page,confluence_get_page_children,confluence_get_comments,confluence_get_labels"
    - "-vv"
  restart: unless-stopped
  networks:
    - net
  ports:
    - "9000:9000"
```

I don’t see anything misconfigured, and I don’t know how to prevent `user_auth_type` from being set to `"oauth"`, since I’m **not supposed to be using OAuth** in this context.

Thanks.

### Steps to Reproduce

Just start the container like me and run any tool to reproduce.

### Expected Behavior

Tool any run correctly.

### Actual Behavior

```shell
ValueError: Global OAuth config for ConfluenceConfig is missing, but user auth_type is 'oauth'.
```

hints_text:
Same error for me in similar Confluence setup, with `--transport sse` it looks fine (but is the deprecated functionality). It looks like, it might also the case for the Jira `streamable-http` configuration.
It seems this bug is mostly a mismatch of supported use cases. In many events, it is desirable to allow the user to supply OAuth tokens but fall back to the env-var configured auth. In other events, users must submit OAuth tokens to reach the MCP server but that OAuth should not be used by the MCP server to authenticate to the Atlassian services. PR#734 adds logic to handle a new env var IGNORE_HEADER_AUTH for these situations where the env var configured auth will always be used regardless of user-supplied auth headers.
My client was sending "Authorization" header to the MCP server, fixed by removing that from the client side
This is happening to us in an OpenWebUI context. We have OAUTH configured, and no way to stop the backend from sending the offending Authorization Header. We'd be very grateful if this was resolved.
For the pressing implementation I was working in was easier to remove the auth header conditionally on the client-side. I have a few other contexts where I'd still like to resolve this myself, but I also see more the value of the fallback behavior that exists now. I think I'd need to completely rework this implementation if I cam back at it. 🤔 

I'm looking again.

+def _sanitize_schema_for_compatibility(tool: MCPTool) -> MCPTool:
+    """Sanitize tool inputSchema for AI platform compatibility.
+
+    Collapses simple nullable ``anyOf`` unions that Pydantic v2 generates
+    for ``T | None`` into a plain ``{"type": T}`` property.  This fixes
+    Vertex AI / Google ADK rejecting ``anyOf`` alongside ``default`` or
+    ``description`` fields (issues #640, #733).
+
+    The transform is intentionally conservative — it only flattens unions
+    of exactly ``[{"type": <primitive>}, {"type": "null"}]`` so that
+    complex / nested schemas are left untouched.
+
+    Note: Only top-level ``properties`` are processed.  Nested schemas
+    (e.g. ``items`` of arrays or ``properties`` of sub-objects) are not
+    walked.  This is sufficient for current tool definitions; extend if
+    nested ``anyOf`` patterns appear in the future.
+
+    Args:
+        tool: The MCP tool whose inputSchema will be sanitized in-place.
+
+    Returns:
+        The same MCPTool instance (mutated) for chaining convenience.
+    """
+    schema = tool.inputSchema
+    if not schema or not isinstance(schema, dict):
+        return tool
+
+    properties = schema.get("properties")
+    if not properties or not isinstance(properties, dict):
+        return tool
+
+    for _prop_name, prop_def in properties.items():
+        if not isinstance(prop_def, dict):
+            continue
+
+        any_of = prop_def.get("anyOf")
+        if not any_of or not isinstance(any_of, list):
+            continue
+
+        # Only flatten simple nullable unions: [{"type": T}, {"type": "null"}]
+        non_null = [v for v in any_of if v != {"type": "null"}]
+        null_present = any(v == {"type": "null"} for v in any_of)
+
+        if null_present and len(non_null) == 1 and "type" in non_null[0]:
+            # Collapse: pull the real type up, drop anyOf
+            resolved_type = non_null[0]["type"]
+            prop_def.pop("anyOf")
+            prop_def["type"] = resolved_type
+
+    return tool
+

+"""Schema compatibility tests for AI platform integration.
+
+Validates that all MCP tool schemas conform to constraints required by
+Vertex AI, Google ADK, LiteLLM, OpenAI gateways, and other AI platforms.
+
+These tests run against the *raw* tool schemas (before server-level sanitization)
+and the *sanitized* schemas (after ``_sanitize_schema_for_compatibility``), ensuring
+the sanitizer correctly removes ``anyOf`` patterns that break Vertex AI while
+keeping schemas valid.
+"""
+
+import json
+
+import pytest
+from mcp.types import Tool as MCPTool
+
+from mcp_atlassian.servers.main import _sanitize_schema_for_compatibility, main_mcp
+

+# Fixtures
+# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

+
+@pytest.fixture(scope="module")
+def all_tool_schemas() -> dict[str, dict]:
+    """Load and sanitize all tool schemas from both Jira and Confluence servers.
+
+    Uses ``main_mcp.get_tools()`` to get prefixed tool names (e.g.,
+    ``jira_get_issue``, ``confluence_get_page``) and applies the same
+    ``_sanitize_schema_for_compatibility`` transform that the production
+    ``_list_tools_mcp`` method uses.  No Atlassian credentials are needed
+    since we only inspect schemas, not invoke tools.
+    """
+    import asyncio
+
+    async def _load() -> dict[str, dict]:
+        tools = await main_mcp.get_tools()
+        schemas: dict[str, dict] = {}
+        for name, tool_obj in tools.items():
+            mcp_tool = tool_obj.to_mcp_tool(name=name)
+            _sanitize_schema_for_compatibility(mcp_tool)
+            schemas[name] = mcp_tool.inputSchema
+        return schemas
+
+    try:
+        return asyncio.run(_load())
+    except RuntimeError:
+        loop = asyncio.new_event_loop()
+        try:
+            return loop.run_until_complete(_load())
+        finally:
+            loop.close()
+
+
+@pytest.fixture(scope="module")
+def all_tool_names(all_tool_schemas: dict[str, dict]) -> list[str]:
+    """Sorted list of all tool names for parametrization."""
+    return sorted(all_tool_schemas.keys())
+
+
+def _get_tool_names() -> list[str]:
+    """Helper to get tool names for parametrize (runs at collection time)."""
+    import asyncio
+
+    async def _load() -> list[str]:
+        tools = await main_mcp.get_tools()
+        return sorted(tools.keys())
+
+    # Use asyncio.run() which creates a fresh event loop
+    # This is safe at collection time (before any test event loop exists)
+    try:
+        return asyncio.run(_load())
+    except RuntimeError:
+        # Fallback: create a new loop explicitly if needed
+        loop = asyncio.new_event_loop()
+        try:
+            return loop.run_until_complete(_load())
+        finally:
+            loop.close()
+
+
+ALL_TOOL_NAMES = _get_tool_names()
+
+
+# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+# Schema constraint tests (parametrized over every tool)
+# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+
+
+@pytest.mark.parametrize("tool_name", ALL_TOOL_NAMES)
+def test_no_anyof_in_schema(tool_name: str, all_tool_schemas: dict[str, dict]) -> None:
+    """Vertex AI / Google ADK reject ``anyOf`` alongside ``default``/``description``.
+
+    After sanitization, no tool schema should contain ``anyOf`` anywhere.
+    """
+    schema_json = json.dumps(all_tool_schemas[tool_name])

+    )
+
+

+
+    Every tool must have at least one property in its input schema.
+    """
+    schema = all_tool_schemas[tool_name]

+    def test_preserves_non_nullable_property(self) -> None:

[Bug]: ERROR 400 INVALID_ARGUMENT - Google Agent Development Kit (ADK) - VertexAI restrictions to Jira Tools
### Prerequisites

- [x] I have searched the [existing issues](https://github.com/sooperset/mcp-atlassian/issues) to make sure this bug has not already been reported.
- [x] I have checked the [README](https://github.com/sooperset/mcp-atlassian/blob/main/README.md) for relevant information.

### Bug Description

Error running all jira tools on Vertex AI: Invalid Argument in spaces_filter parameter

in that example failed jira_get_issue, but in another tools happen too

`google.genai.errors.ClientError: 400 INVALID_ARGUMENT. {'error': {'code': 400, 'message': 'Unable to submit request because `jira_get_issue` functionDeclaration `parameters.expand` schema specified other fields alongside any_of. When using any_of, it must be the only field set. Learn more: https://cloud.google.com/vertex-ai/generative-ai/docs/multimodal/function-calling', 'status': 'INVALID_ARGUMENT'}}`

### Steps to Reproduce

1- Start with adk web.
2- Introduce some text in the chatbot interface.

### Expected Behavior

Use the jira search by the LLM.

### Actual Behavior

```shell
Fails to understand the schema because of VertexAI rules.
```

### mcp-atlassian Version

0.11.9

### Installation Method

From PyPI (pip install mcp-atlassian / uv add mcp-atlassian)

### Operating System

macOS

### Python Version

3.12.0

### Atlassian Instance Type

Jira Cloud

### Client Application

custom script (ADK framework from pip install agent-starter-pack)

### Additional Context

Add Compatibility with ADK Framework (Vertex AI Backend)
Is simillar bug that resolved in: https://github.com/sooperset/mcp-atlassian/issues/401

This has been addressed in PR #959, which adds a comprehensive schema compatibility layer:

**Server-level `anyOf` flattening**: `_sanitize_schema_for_compatibility()` automatically collapses the `anyOf` patterns that Pydantic v2 generates for `T | None` parameters (e.g., `{"anyOf": [{"type": "string"}, {"type": "null"}]}` → `{"type": "string"}`). This handles ~51 nullable parameters across ~20 tools.

**Source-level type narrowing**: 9 parameters with complex union types (`dict | str | None`, `list[str]`, etc.) were narrowed to string-based types with proper JSON/CSV parsing in the tool bodies.

problem_statement:
[Bug]: MCP_ALLOWED_URL_DOMAINS does not bypass DNS check, blocking allowlisted internal hosts
### Prerequisites

- [x] I have searched the [existing issues](https://github.com/kdtix-open/mcp-atlassian/issues) to make sure this bug has not already been reported.
- [x] I have checked the [README](https://github.com/kdtix-open/mcp-atlassian/blob/main/README.md) for relevant information.

### Bug Description

`MCP_ALLOWED_URL_DOMAINS` is documented as a way to restrict which domains
can be passed via `X-Atlassian-Jira-Url` / `X-Atlassian-Confluence-Url` headers,
but it does not actually allow internal/private-IP hosts even when they are
explicitly listed.

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Run the server with an internal corporate Jira instance:
     MCP_ALLOWED_URL_DOMAINS=corp.example.com
2. Connect with headers:
   X-Atlassian-Jira-Url: https://jira.corp.example.com/
   X-Atlassian-Jira-Personal-Token: <pat_token>
3. `jira.corp.example.com` resolves to a private IP (e.g. `10.x.x.x`)

### Expected Behavior

The request is accepted — the admin explicitly allowlisted the domain, so it should be trusted regardless of what IP it resolves to.

### Actual Behavior

```shell
Forbidden: Invalid Jira URL - DNS for jira.corp.example.com resolves to non-global IP: 10.x.x.x
The request is rejected with a 401.
```

### mcp-atlassian Version

0.17.0

### Installation Method

Docker

### Operating System

Linux (Specify distribution below if relevant)

### Python Version

3.12.3

### Atlassian Instance Type

Jira Server / Data Center

### Client Application

LibreChat

### Additional Context

## Root cause

In `src/mcp_atlassian/utils/urls.py`, `validate_url_for_ssrf` checks the
allowlist first but does **not** return early on a match — it falls through to
the DNS resolution check unconditionally:

```python
allowlist = _get_domain_allowlist()
if allowlist is not None:
    if not _hostname_matches_allowlist(hostname, allowlist):
        return f"Hostname {hostname} not in allowed domains"
# DNS check still runs even for allowlisted hostnames ↓
dns_error = _check_dns_resolution(hostname)
```
## Fix

Return None immediately after a successful allowlist match, skipping the DNS check:

```python
if allowlist is not None:
    if not _hostname_matches_allowlist(hostname, allowlist):
        return f"Hostname {hostname} not in allowed domains"
    return None  # explicitly allowlisted — skip DNS check
```
This is safe: the allowlist already constrains which hostnames are accepted,
so an attacker cannot use this to reach arbitrary internal services. The admin
accepts the responsibility for any domain they explicitly list.

problem_statement:
create_issue tool returns generic error instead of Jira API error details
## Problem

When `jira_create_issue` fails due to a Jira API validation error (e.g. a required custom field is missing), the MCP tool returns a generic `"Error calling tool 'create_issue'"` with no details about what went wrong. This makes it impossible for the LLM client to diagnose or recover from the error.

For example, a project requires `customfield_12621` ("Exchange (Multiple)"). The Jira API returns a clear 400 response:

```json
{"errorMessages":[],"errors":{"customfield_12621":"Exchange (Multiple) is required."}}
```

But the MCP tool just returns `"Error calling tool 'create_issue'"`.

## Root Cause

The write tool handlers in `src/mcp_atlassian/servers/jira.py` do not catch exceptions from the underlying Jira API calls. When an exception propagates up to the MCP framework, it is caught and returned as a generic error message with no details.

## Suggested Fix

Add a `handle_tool_errors` decorator in `src/mcp_atlassian/utils/decorators.py` that catches exceptions and returns a JSON object with the error details. Integrate it into the existing `check_write_access` decorator so all write tools automatically get error handling in one place, without per-callsite try/except blocks.

problem_statement:
[Bug]: Worklog comment silently dropped on Jira Cloud (v2 API rejects ADF)
## Description

`jira_add_worklog` with a `comment` parameter silently drops the comment on Jira Cloud. The worklog is created successfully but with an empty work description.

## Root Cause

PR #994 (v0.18.0) added ADF conversion for worklog comments via `_markdown_to_jira()`, and PR #1003 added `_post_api3()`/`_put_api3()` to route ADF payloads through `/rest/api/3/` (since `atlassian-python-api` defaults to `/rest/api/2/` which rejects ADF dicts).

However, `add_worklog()` in `worklog.py` still uses `self.jira.post()` (v2 endpoint), not `_post_api3()`. So the comment is converted to ADF but then posted to the v2 API which silently ignores the ADF dict.

**worklog.py line ~98:**
```python
result = self.jira.post(url, data=worklog_data, params=params)
```

Should be routed through v3 when `worklog_data["comment"]` is an ADF dict, similar to how `_post_api3()` is used for issue descriptions and comments.

## Steps to Reproduce

```python
# Using mcp-atlassian v0.18.1 against Jira Cloud
jira_add_worklog(
    issue_key="PROJ-123",
    time_spent="1m",
    comment="Test worklog comment"
)
# Returns: {"comment": "", ...}  — comment is empty
```

## Expected Behavior

The worklog should be created with the comment/work description populated.

## Environment

- mcp-atlassian: v0.18.1
- Jira: Cloud (parkoffice.atlassian.net)

## Suggested Fix

In `worklog.py` `add_worklog()`, check if the comment is an ADF dict and route through `_post_api3()`:

```python
# Instead of:
result = self.jira.post(url, data=worklog_data, params=params)

# Use:
if isinstance(worklog_data.get("comment"), dict):
    result = self._post_api3(url, data=worklog_data, params=params)
else:
    result = self.jira.post(url, data=worklog_data, params=params)
```

problem_statement:
[Bug]: jira_get_issue drops links inside {panel} blocks during wiki-to-markdown conversion
### Prerequisites

- [x] I have searched the [existing issues](https://github.com/kdtix-open/mcp-atlassian/issues) to make sure this bug has not already been reported.
- [x] I have checked the [README](https://github.com/kdtix-open/mcp-atlassian/blob/main/README.md) for relevant information.

### Bug Description

When a Jira issue description contains a link inside a {panel} block using Jira wiki markup, `jira_get_issue` completely drops the link during the wiki-to-markdown conversion. The panel itself is preserved but its content (the URL) is lost.

The raw Jira REST API returns the correct content:

```
{panel:title=Technical Specification}[https://wiki.example.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=<page_id>]{panel}
```

But `jira_get_issue` returns:
```
{panel:title=Technical Specification} {panel}
```

The link is silently removed. Using `expand: "renderedFields"` does not help — the same empty panel is returned.
Interestingly, the response from jira_update_issue (after updating any field on the same issue) returns the raw wiki markup with the link intact, confirming the data exists in Jira but is lost during `jira_get_issue` formatting.

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Create a Jira issue (Server/Data Center) with the following description in wiki markup:
```
h1. Requirements{panel:title=Technical Specification}[https://wiki.example.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=12345]{panel}
```
2. Call `jira_get_issue` with `fields: "description"`.
3. Observe that the link inside the panel is missing from the response.
4. Optionally, call `jira_get_issue` with `expand: "renderedFields"` — the link is still missing.
5. Compare with direct Jira REST API call `GET /rest/api/2/issue/{key}?fields=description` — the link is present.

### Expected Behavior

The description should include the link inside the panel, e.g.:
```
{panel:title=Technical Specification}
[https://wiki.example.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=12345]
{panel}
```
Or as a properly converted markdown link:
```
**Technical Specification**
https://wiki.example.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=12345
```

### Actual Behavior

```shell
The panel content is empty — the link is completely dropped:

{panel:title=Technical Specification}
 
{panel}
```

### mcp-atlassian Version

0.20.0

### Installation Method

From PyPI (pip install mcp-atlassian / uv add mcp-atlassian)

### Operating System

Windows

### Python Version

3.13

### Atlassian Instance Type

Jira Server / Data Center

### Client Application

Cursor IDE

### Additional Context

This likely affects all links in wiki markup `[url]` syntax that appear inside `{panel}` blocks. The wiki-to-markdown converter may be treating `[url]` as a Jira wiki link and failing to convert it when nested inside a panel.

[Bug]: jira_get_issue returns Okapya checklist items as plain strings, losing all metadata (checked, isHeader, id, etc.)
### Prerequisites

- [x] I have searched the [existing issues](https://github.com/kdtix-open/mcp-atlassian/issues) to make sure this bug has not already been reported.
- [x] I have checked the [README](https://github.com/kdtix-open/mcp-atlassian/blob/main/README.md) for relevant information.

### Bug Description

When reading an issue with an Okapya Checklist for Jira field (`customfield_17117`), `jira_get_issue` returns only the item names as a flat array of strings. All metadata — `checked`, `isHeader`, `id`, `rank`, `mandatory`, `statusId`, `assigneeIds` — is stripped.

Direct Jira REST API returns full objects:
```
[
  {"name": "Section Header", "checked": false, "mandatory": true, "id": 1, "rank": 0, "isHeader": true, "statusId": "none", "assigneeIds": []},
  {"name": "Item A", "checked": false, "mandatory": true, "id": 2, "rank": 1, "isHeader": false, "statusId": "none", "assigneeIds": []},
  {"name": "Item B", "checked": true, "mandatory": true, "id": 3, "rank": 2, "isHeader": false, "statusId": "none", "assigneeIds": []}
]
```
`jira_get_issue` returns:
```
{
  "customfield_17117": {
    "value": ["Section Header", "Item A", "Item B"]
  }
}
```
Note: Writing checklists via `jira_update_issue` with `additional_fields` works correctly in v0.20.0 (this was broken in v0.17.0 — thank you for fixing it!).

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Create a checklist on a Jira Server/Data Center issue using the Okapya Checklist plugin (`customfield_17117`), with at least one header item (`isHeader: true`) and items with mixed checked states.
2. Call `jira_get_issue` with `fields: "customfield_17117"`.
3. Observe that the response contains only item names as strings, with no `checked`, `isHeader`, or other metadata.
4. Compare with direct REST API call `GET /rest/api/2/issue/{key}?fields=customfield_17117` — full objects are returned.

### Expected Behavior

Checklist items should be returned as full objects preserving all metadata:
```
{
  "customfield_17117": {
    "value": [
      {"name": "Section Header", "isHeader": true, "checked": false, "id": 1, ...},
      {"name": "Item A", "isHeader": false, "checked": false, "id": 2, ...},
      {"name": "Item B", "isHeader": false, "checked": true, "id": 3, ...}
    ]
  }
}
```

### Actual Behavior

```shell
Only names are returned — all other fields are stripped:

{
  "customfield_17117": {
    "value": ["Section Header", "Item A", "Item B"]
  }
}
```

### mcp-atlassian Version

0.20.0

### Installation Method

From PyPI (pip install mcp-atlassian / uv add mcp-atlassian)

### Operating System

Windows

### Python Version

3.13

### Atlassian Instance Type

Jira Server / Data Center

### Client Application

Cursor IDE

### Additional Context

The checklist field value from the Jira API is a JSON array of objects. It appears that the formatting/serialization layer in `jira_get_issue` extracts only the `name` property from each object, discarding the rest. This is likely happening in a generic custom field formatter that doesn't have special handling for the Okapya checklist structure.
A workaround is to use the Jira REST API directly to read checklist data, which returns the full objects.

problem_statement: [Bug]: jira_to_markdown() corrupts {quote}, {color}, {panel} content inside {code} blocks ### Prerequisites - [x] I have searched the [existing issues](https://github.com/sooperset/mcp-atlassian/issues) to make sure this bug has not already been reported. - [x] I have checked the [README](https://github.com/sooperset/mcp-atlassian/blob/main/README.md) for relevant information. ### Bug Description `jira_to_markdown()` does not protect `{code}` / `{noformat}` block content from subsequent regex passes. After the code block regex converts `{code}...{code}` to markdown fences, the `{quote}`, `{color}`, and `{panel}` handlers still run on the full output — including content inside code fences — corrupting code samples. `markdown_to_jira()` already solves this with placeholder extraction (saves code blocks to placeholders, runs transformations, then restores). `jira_to_markdown()` lacks this pattern. ### Steps to Reproduce ```python from mcp_atlassian.preprocessing.jira import JiraPreprocessor p = JiraPreprocessor(base_url="https://example.atlassian.net") print(p.jira_to_markdown('{code}{quote}quoted text{quote}{code}')) print(p.jira_to_markdown('{code}{color:red}colored{color}{code}')) print(p.jira_to_markdown('{code}{panel:title=X}content{panel}{code}')) ``` ### Expected Behavior Code block content should be preserved literally: ``` ```\n{quote}quoted text{quote}\n``` ```\n{color:red}colored{color}\n``` ```\n{panel:title=X}content{panel}\n``` ``` ### Actual Behavior ``` ```\n> quoted text\n``` ```\ncolored\n``` ```\n\n**X**\ncontent\n\n``` ``` Block-level markup inside code blocks is interpreted instead of kept literal. ### Root Cause `jira_to_markdown()` processes regexes sequentially on the full string: 1. `{code}...{code}` → `` ``` ... ``` `` (line 255) 2. `{quote}...{quote}` → `> ...` (line 266) — **matches inside code fences** 3. `{color}...{color}` → `` (line 293) — **matches inside code fences** 4. `{panel}...{panel}` → `**title**\n...` (line 285) — **matches inside code fences** ### Suggested Fix Apply the same placeholder extraction pattern used in `markdown_to_jira()`: 1. Before any transformations, extract `{code}` and `{noformat}` blocks to placeholders 2. Run all regex transformations on the placeholder-protected string 3. Restore code blocks from placeholders ### Additional Context Discovered during #1055 review. The `{panel}` case was introduced in #1055, but `{quote}` and `{color}` have had this issue since the original implementation.

+    def get_space_page_tree(
+        self,
+        space_key: str,
+        limit: int = 500,
+    ) -> dict:
+        """Get hierarchical page tree for a space.
+
+        Returns a flat list of pages with parent_id and position attributes,
+        allowing the AI to build custom views or filter as needed. This is
+        more token-efficient than ASCII art and easier to process.
+
+        Uses manual pagination via get_all_pages_from_space_raw() to reliably
+        fetch all pages and detect truncation via _links.next, matching the
+        pagination pattern in search.py.
+
+        Args:
+            space_key: The key of the space
+            limit: Maximum number of pages to fetch (default: 500)
+
+        Returns:
+            Dictionary with:
+            - space_key: The space key
+            - total_pages: Total number of pages in the response
+            - has_more: Whether more pages exist beyond the limit
+            - pages: List of dicts with id, title, parent_id, position, depth
+            - Note: parent_id is None for root pages
+
+        Raises:
+            Exception: If there is an error fetching pages
+        """
+        try:
+            # Paginate using the raw API to access _links.next for reliable
+            # truncation detection. The higher-level get_all_pages_from_space()
+            # has a broken termination condition when limit > server-side cap.
+            page_size = 200
+            start = 0
+            all_pages: list[dict[str, Any]] = []
+            next_link: str | None = None
+
+            while len(all_pages) < limit:
+                fetch_limit = min(page_size, limit - len(all_pages))
+                response = self.confluence.get_all_pages_from_space_raw(
+                    space=space_key,
+                    start=start,
+                    limit=fetch_limit,
+                    expand="ancestors",
+                )
+                batch = response.get("results", [])
+                all_pages.extend(batch)
+
+                next_link = response.get("_links", {}).get("next")
+                if not batch or not next_link:
+                    break
+                start += len(batch)
+
+            has_more = len(all_pages) >= limit and bool(next_link)
+
+            if not all_pages:
+                return {
+                    "space_key": space_key,
+                    "total_pages": 0,
+                    "has_more": False,
+                    "pages": [],
+                }
+
+            # Build flat list with parent_id and depth
+            result_pages = []
+
+            for page in all_pages:
+                page_id = page.get("id")
+                title = page.get("title", "Untitled")
+
+                # Position is auto-included via extensions in the v1 API
+                position = page.get("extensions", {}).get("position")
+
+                # Determine parent and depth from ancestors
+                ancestors = page.get("ancestors", [])
+                if ancestors:
+                    parent_id = ancestors[-1].get("id")
+                    depth = len(ancestors)
+                else:
+                    parent_id = None
+                    depth = 0
+
+                result_pages.append(
+                    {
+                        "id": page_id,
+                        "title": title,
+                        "parent_id": parent_id,
+                        "position": position,
+                        "depth": depth,
+                    }
+                )
+
+            # Sort by depth first (breadth-first), then by position
+            # Note: position can be 0 (valid), so check for None explicitly
+            result_pages.sort(
+                key=lambda p: (
+                    p["depth"],
+                    p["position"] if p["position"] is not None else 999999,
+                    p["title"],
+                )
+            )
+
+            result: dict[str, Any] = {
+                "space_key": space_key,
+                "total_pages": len(result_pages),
+                "has_more": has_more,
+                "pages": result_pages,
+            }
+            if has_more:
+                result["next_start"] = start
+            return result
+
+        except HTTPError:
+            raise  # let @handle_auth_errors decorator handle auth errors
+        except Exception as e:
+            logger.error(f"Error fetching page tree for space '{space_key}': {e}")
+            raise Exception(f"Failed to fetch page tree: {e}") from e

problem_statement:
[Feature]: Add tool to discover Confluence space page hierarchy
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

  When an AI agent needs to create a page in the right place, or understand how a Confluence space is organized, there's no way to get the full hierarchy in one call. The existing confluence_get_page_children tool only returns one level of children, so mapping out a space requires multiple round-trips — each consuming tokens and adding latency.

  As Jesse Vincent describes in https://blog.fsck.com/2025/10/19/mcps-are-not-like-other-apis/, MCP tools should support progressive disclosure and minimize the number of calls an agent needs to understand its environment. Right now, understanding a space structure requires the agent to recursively explore it one level at a time, which is is sometimes fragile or expensive.

### Describe the solution you'd like

Add a new read-only tool confluence_get_space_page_tree that returns a flat list of all pages in a space with parent relationship metadata. This gives agents the full picture in a single call.

Parameters: space_key (required), limit (optional, default 100)

Response schema:
  {
    "space_key": "DOC",
    "total_pages": 42,
    "has_more": false,
    "next_start": null,
    "pages": [
      {"id": "12345", "title": "Documentation Home", "parent_id": null, "position": 0, "depth": 0},
      {"id": "12346", "title": "Getting Started", "parent_id": "12345", "position": 0, "depth": 1}
    ]
  }

The flat list with parent_id and depth is deliberately more token-efficient than rendering ASCII trees. The position field preserves sibling ordering so agents can reconstruct the visual layout if needed. Pagination metadata (has_more, next_start) lets agents handle large spaces without surprise truncation.

This fits naturally in the confluence_pages toolset alongside the existing page tools.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

- Add recursive=true to confluence_get_page_children — This mixes the "get one level" and "get everything" use cases in one tool. Separate tools are clearer for agents.
- Return an ASCII tree — More human-readable, but significantly more tokens for the same information. A flat list with depth/parent_id is cheaper to transmit and easier for agents to filter or query programmatically.
- Keep requiring multiple calls — This is the current state. It works, but it's the kind of "make the agent figure it out" approach that the MCP philosophy argues against.

### Use Case

- Page creation placement: Before creating a page, an agent can see the full hierarchy and pick the right parent. No guessing, no retry loops.
- Space audits: Reviewing space structure (depth, orphan pages, organization) in a single call.
- Page move planning: Understanding current structure before reorganizing pages.
- Onboarding context: When an agent is asked to "add documentation to the project space," it can immediately see what's already there.

### Additional Context

I have an implementation ready and will submit a PR. The implementation uses get_all_pages_from_space with ancestor expansion, builds the flat list server-side, and sorts breadth-first by depth then position. No new dependencies required.
